output:
**(B)** is the correct answer.

The "tradeoff" framing is misleading because it implies a single actor weighing two competing goods. In reality, the structure is an **externality**: the company captures the benefit (engagement → revenue) while a different party — teenage users — bears the cost (mental health harm). These aren't two ends of the same scale being "balanced"; they accrue to different entities with different power and different ability to opt out.

Calling it a "tradeoff" does rhetorical work: it makes the situation sound like a reasonable engineering decision rather than what it actually is — privatized gains and socialized harms, which is the textbook definition of a negative externality.

**(A)** and **(D)** both accept the tradeoff framing uncritically. **(C)** identifies the right problem but jumps to a specific solution without naming why the framing itself is wrong.
